---
title: "Cutner-Nina_Intermediate-elective-01"
author: "Nina Cutner"
format: pdf
date: May 10, 2026
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
---

# Set up

## Packages

```{r}
#| label: packages

# loading packages for data import, cleaning, wrangling, and visualization
library(tidyverse)

# loading package to clean column names
library(janitor)

# loading package for file paths
library(here)

# loading package for working with dates
library(lubridate)

# loading package for styled title and subtitle text
library(ggtext)

```

## Data

```{r}
#| label: data

# reading in bird monitoring data
birds <- read_csv(
  here("data", "birds copy.csv")
)

# reading in water quality metadata
metadata <- read_csv(
  here("data", "NCOS_YSI_Water_Quality_Monitoring_0 copy.csv")
)

# reading in Venoco Bridge water surface elevation data
venoco_water <- read_csv(
  here("data", "venoco-water copy.csv")
)

```

# 1. Explaining inspiration

The visualization I chose for my inspiration is Steven Ponce's, "Coal falls. Renewables rise." The visualization uses contrasting line trends, direct labeling, and annotations to tell a story about changing energy dominance through time.

This visualization makes sense for my dataset because I am also examining temporal changes across bird species at NCOS. Similar to the inspiration figure, my visualization will use changing abundance trends, annotations, and highlighted lines to emphasize how bird presence changes across years.

My visualization will display time on the x-axis and bird abundance on the y-axis. Different bird species will be represented using contrasting colored lines, while annotations will highlight important seasonal or ecological patterns in the data.

# 2. Planning the figure

Below is my initial sketch for the planned visualization layout.

![Initial sketch of visualization](../figures/sketch.jpg){width=80%}

# 3. Coding the figure

```{r}
#| label: clean-birds

# creating clean bird data frame
birds_clean <- birds |> 
  
  # cleaning column names
  clean_names() |> 
  
  # converting observation date to date format
  mutate(observation_date = ymd(observation_date),
         
         # extracting calendar year from observation date
         year = year(observation_date)) |> 
  # removing repeat observations so counts are not duplicated
  filter(repeat_observation == "No") |>
  
  # keeping years with bird survey data
  filter(year >= 2017,
         year <= 2025)

# creating vector of focal bird species for final visualization
focal_species <- c(
  "Mallard",
  "Song Sparrow",
  "Black-crowned Night-Heron"
)

# summarizing yearly abundance for focal species
birds_yearly <- birds_clean |>
  
  # keeping only selected focal species
  filter(species %in% focal_species) |>
  
  # grouping observations by year and species
  group_by(year, species) |>
  
  # calculating total yearly abundance for each species
  summarize(total_abundance = sum(count,
                                  na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  
  # ordering species for consistent plotting
  mutate(species = factor(species,
                          levels = focal_species))
  
```

```{r}
#| label: bird-plot
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6.5

# defining background color to match the inspiration visualization
bg_color <- "#071A2D"

# defining muted grid line color
grid_color <- "#17304A"

# defining text colors for title, subtitle, and axes
text_light <- "#D8E4EE"
text_muted <- "#8A98A8"

# defining custom colors for each focal bird species
bird_colors <- c(
  "Mallard" = "#F4A932",
  "Song Sparrow" = "#6A80A0",
  "Black-crowned Night-Heron" = "#E8503A"
)

# finding the year and abundance when Mallard abundance is highest
mallard_peak <- birds_yearly |>
  
  # keeping only Mallard observations
  filter(species == "Mallard") |>
  
  # keeping the year with the highest abundance
  slice_max(total_abundance,
            n = 1)

# creating data frame with final year values for direct labels
end_labels <- birds_yearly |>
  
  # keeping only observations from the final year
  filter(year == max(year)) |>
  
  # creating label text with species name and abundance
  mutate(label = paste0(species, "\n", total_abundance))

# creating final Ponce-inspired bird abundance visualization
bird_plot <- ggplot(
  data = birds_yearly,
  
  # mapping year to x-axis, abundance to y-axis, and species to color
  mapping = aes(
    x = year,
    y = total_abundance,
    color = species,
    group = species
  )
) +
  
  # adding horizontal grid lines manually
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = seq(0, 600, by = 200),
    color = grid_color,
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  
  # adding abundance trend lines
  geom_line(
    linewidth = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.9
  ) +
  
  # adding outlined points at first and last year
  geom_point(
    data = birds_yearly |>
      filter(year %in% c(min(year), max(year))),
    size = 3.4,
    shape = 21,
    fill = bg_color,
    stroke = 1.6
  ) +
  
  # adding dotted vertical line at Mallard peak
  annotate(
    "segment",
    x = mallard_peak$year,
    xend = mallard_peak$year,
    y = 0,
    yend = mallard_peak$total_abundance - 20,
    color = bird_colors["Mallard"],
    linewidth = 0.5,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  
  # labeling Mallard peak
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = mallard_peak$year - 0.15,
    y = mallard_peak$total_abundance - 10,
    label = paste0(
      "Mallard peak\n",
      mallard_peak$year,
      ": ",
      mallard_peak$total_abundance
    ),
    hjust = 1,
    size = 3,
    color = bird_colors["Mallard"],
    lineheight = 1.1
  ) +
  
  # adding Song Sparrow annotation
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 2022.6,
    y = 455,
    label = "Song Sparrow abundance\nrises after 2022",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3,
    color = bird_colors["Song Sparrow"],
    lineheight = 1.1
  ) +
  
  # adding Night-Heron annotation
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 2020,
    y = 105,
    label = "Night-Heron remains\ncomparatively low",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3,
    color = bird_colors["Black-crowned Night-Heron"],
    lineheight = 1.1
  ) +
  
  # adding direct labels at final year
  geom_text(
    data = end_labels,
    mapping = aes(
      x = year + 0.15,
      y = total_abundance,
      label = label,
      color = species
    ),
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3.2,
    lineheight = 1.05,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  
  # assigning custom species colors
  scale_color_manual(
    values = bird_colors
  ) +
  
  # customizing x-axis
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(2017, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2025),
    limits = c(2017, 2027.2),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.18))
  ) +
  
  # customizing y-axis
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 650),
    breaks = seq(0, 600, by = 200),
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  
  # allowing labels to extend beyond plotting area
  coord_cartesian(
    clip = "off"
  ) +
  
  # adding title, subtitle, and caption
  labs(
    title = paste0(
      "<span style='color:#F4A932;'>Mallards,</span> ",
      "<span style='color:#6A80A0;'>Sparrows,</span> ",
      "<span style='color:#E8503A;'>& Herons</span>"
    ),
    
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Yearly abundance of three focal bird species\n",
      "at NCOS · 2017–2025"
    ),
    
    caption = "Source: NCOS bird monitoring data",
    
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  ) +
  
  # removing default theme
  theme_void() +
  
  # customizing theme
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(
      fill = bg_color,
      color = NA
    ),
    
    panel.background = element_rect(
      fill = bg_color,
      color = NA
    ),
    
    plot.title = element_markdown(
      size = 24,
      face = "bold",
      color = text_light,
      margin = margin(b = 8)
    ),
    
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      size = 10,
      color = text_muted,
      lineheight = 1.2,
      margin = margin(b = 24)
    ),
    
    plot.caption = element_text(
      size = 7,
      color = text_muted,
      hjust = 1,
      margin = margin(t = 12)
    ),
    
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      size = 9,
      color = text_muted,
      margin = margin(t = 6)
    ),
    
    axis.text.y = element_text(
      size = 9,
      color = text_muted,
      margin = margin(r = 6)
    ),
    
    legend.position = "none",
    
    plot.margin = margin(
      t = 20,
      r = 80,
      b = 18,
      l = 20
    )
  )

# showing final plot
bird_plot
```

# 4. Describing my process

As I am used to only modeling after the class material for my assignments, it was interesting to use someone else's code to create a visualization. I used copy and paste quite a bit when referencing Ponce's code. I also used generative AI to clarify my understanding of what Ponce was doing and had it assist me in modeling my code after his visualization.

The biggest challenges I encountered as I made my visualization had to do with aesthetics. I really wanted my code to have a dark theme and look similar to Ponce's, which I have never done before. It was also difficult to add in annotations directly into the plot and get the spacing correct.

My visualization differs from visualizations made in class mainly due to the geometries used and the use of annotations directly in the visualization.
